label: Geodesic
definition: The straightest possible path between two points on a curved surface or manifold, generalizing the idea of a straight line to spaces where ordinary straight lines do not exist.

body.text: A geodesic is the curved-space analogue of a straight line. On a flat plane the shortest route between two points is a straight segment, but on a curved surface, like the face of the Earth, no straight line lies within the surface at all. The geodesic is the path that stays on the surface and is locally as straight as the geometry allows. For a sphere these are the great circles, which is why flight paths between distant cities bow toward the poles rather than following a constant compass bearing.

Formally, a geodesic is a curve that parallel-transports its own tangent vector, meaning it never turns left or right relative to the surface; it only bends as much as the surface forces it to. Its length is measured by the metric tensor, the object that tells you how to compute distances and angles at each point of a manifold. Where the metric varies from place to place, so do the geodesics, and the way they spread apart or converge is exactly what curvature measures.

Geodesics matter in machine learning because high-dimensional data is widely believed to lie on or near a low-dimensional curved manifold, the manifold hypothesis. When that is true, the meaningful distance between two data points is not the straight-line Euclidean distance through the ambient space but the geodesic distance along the data manifold itself. Two images of a rotating object may be far apart in pixel space yet adjacent along the manifold of poses.

This reframing shows up across the field. Methods like Isomap estimate geodesic distances to unfold a manifold into a flatter representation. Interpolating along a geodesic in the latent space of a generative model produces smooth, plausible transitions rather than the blurry averages you get from a naive straight-line blend. And in information geometry, the space of probability distributions is itself a curved manifold whose geodesics, measured by the Fisher information metric, describe the most efficient paths of statistical change.

image.alt: A sphere with a great circle drawn in red and a smaller parallel circle in blue, showing that the geodesic on a sphere is the great circle.
image.caption: On a flat plane the shortest path between two points is a straight line; on a curved surface it is a geodesic. On a sphere every geodesic is a great circle (red), the largest circle through two points, so a small circle (blue) is always the longer detour.
